thread.title: Businesswoman Kelly Loeffler Said to Be Trump's Pick for Ag Secretary | Farming and Agricultural News | lancasterfarming.com
thread.title_full: Businesswoman Kelly Loeffler Said to Be Trump's Pick for Ag Secretary | Farming and Agricultural News | lancasterfarming.com

author: Philip Gruber , pgruber@lancasterfarming.com
title: Businesswoman Kelly Loeffler Said to Be Trump's Pick for Ag Secretary | Farming and Agricultural News | lancasterfarming.com
text:
President-elect Donald Trump is expected to name former Georgia Sen. Kelly Loeffler as his ag secretary, multiple news outlets reported Nov. 22.
Loeffler’s personal [website](https://kellyloeffler.com/biography/) says she grew up on an Illinois grain farm with a feedlot, and was a member of 4-H.
Loeffler proceeded to have a career as a business executive before Gov. Brian Kemp appointed her to fill a Senate vacancy in 2019. Democrat Raphael Warnock defeated her the following year.
In a [video clip](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TeUv6lWfOe8) posted Nov. 22 by the Atlanta Journal-Constitution, Loeffler said she would be a good fit for any position Trump wanted that would advance his agenda.
“I have so much respect for our farm economy right now, which is facing its second year of recession, and you know, I think it’s a real crisis that we’re not talking about right now,” Loeffler said.
Loeffler said most of the bills she passed in Congress related to rural health care.
She also served on the Agriculture Committee and co-sponsored five bills assigned to the committee.
The only one to pass committee, sponsored by Chairman Sen. Pat Roberts, R-Kan., would have supported USDA’s efforts to counter agroterrorism.
In this congressional session, Warnock has been part of 32 Agriculture Committee bills.
Loeffler is the latest Trump pick who has faced ethical questions. She was one of several senators [investigated](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/senate-ethics-committee-kelly-loeffler-insider-trading-investigation-dismissed/) for insider trading in 2020, though the probes against her were dropped.
Shortly after leaving the Senate, Loeffler [sold](https://www.nbcnews.com/news/us-news/wnba-s-atlanta-dream-sale-approved-ending-ownership-former-sen-n1258986) her 50% stake in the Atlanta Dream WNBA team. She alienated players by [opposing](Kelly%20Loeffler%20against%20'Black%20Lives%20Matter'%20on%20WNBA%20gear%20|%2011alive.com) their social justice protests in the wake of police killings of unarmed Black people in 2020.
The Union of Concerned Scientists was among the first groups to [comment](https://www.ucsusa.org/about/news/loeffler-unqualified-lead-usda) on Loeffler’s reported selection. The group questioned her agricultural experience and accused her of being a “yes-woman” for Trump.
Among the other people supposedly under consideration for ag secretary [were](https://www.lancasterfarming.com/farming-news/news/who-will-lead-trump-s-usda/article_b47ce240-a1eb-11ef-8c21-133143b59961.html) Rep. Thomas Massie of Kentucky, whose views on food-safety laws could have frustrated major ag sectors, and Rep. Glenn Thompson of Pennsylvania, who is focused on passing a new [Farm Bill](https://www.lancasterfarming.com/tncms/asset/editorial/d23af59e-a5f4-11ef-9f4c-6f09dd3bc6af/).
It’s not clear how Loeffler would interact with Robert F. Kennedy Jr., Trump’s pick for health and human services secretary. Kennedy, who promotes multiple diet and health notions rejected by scientists, has [sought](https://apnews.com/article/rfk-usda-trump-agriculture-election-fda-c15c96af05f2b0f48521410578f2f7df) to influence food and ag policy.
Like Loeffler, Trump’s first agriculture secretary, Sonny Perdue, was from Georgia. Perdue, now 77, is the chancellor of the University of Georgia system.
Perdue visited all 50 states while he was ag secretary. But he also drew heat for [disrupting](https://www.lancasterfarming.com/farming-news/news/kansas-city-to-get-usda-agencies/article_5b82c88e-8ded-11e9-b74d-5764fd78f68c.html) a USDA research agency by moving its headquarters to Kansas City, and for [spending](https://www.lancasterfarming.com/farming-news/news/study-trade-aid-exceeded-losses-favored-cotton/article_1a84e591-2ed9-5144-8415-406e77ccf034.html) more than $20 billion to compensate farmers for [trade disruptions](https://www.lancasterfarming.com/tncms/asset/editorial/ba4ea030-8bea-11ef-82cb-4b96e908c4c1/) instigated by Trump.
